summary: Joel Spolsky dismantles the common complaint about 'bloatware' by showing that software isn't actually getting bigger in economic terms — Excel 2000 costs $1.03 in hard drive space versus $36 for Excel 5.0 in 1993 dollars. He argues that modern operating systems only load the pages actually used, making executable size largely irrelevant to performance. He then attacks the '80/20 myth' — the belief that you can build a lite product with 20% of features and capture 80% of the market — by pointing out that every user needs a different 20%. Companies that ship lite word processors consistently fail because the missing features are always someone's dealbreaker.
key_points: Software size measured in dollars of hard drive space has actually decreased dramatically over time, making bloatware complaints economically unfounded
Modern operating systems use demand-paged virtual memory, so large executables don't consume resources for unused code paths
The 80/20 rule is misleading for software features because every user relies on a different 20%, so cutting 80% of features alienates nearly everyone
Developer time spent shrinking code size produces imperceptible benefits to users while delaying valuable features
Dozens of companies have tried shipping 'lite' products and consistently failed because real user needs are large and varied
money_quotes: In real terms, it's almost like Excel is actually getting smaller!
Hard drive space is down to $0.0071 per megabyte and still plummeting like a sheep learning to fly by jumping out of a tree.
I'm starting to suspect that fretting about bloatware is more of a mental health problem than a software problem.

If programmers don't have to worry about how large their code is, they can ship it sooner. And that means you get more features, and features make your life better (when you use them) and don't usually hurt (when you don't).

A lot of software developers are seduced by the old '80/20' rule. It seems to make a lot of sense: 80% of the people use 20% of the features. So you convince yourself that you only need to implement 20% of the features, and you can still sell 80% as many copies.
Unfortunately, it's never the same 20%. Everybody uses a different set of features.
Mozilla is not big because it's full of useless crap. Mozilla is big because your needs are big. Your needs are big because the Internet is big.
key_insight: Bloatware complaints ignore the economics of plummeting storage costs, and the 80/20 strategy fails because every user depends on a different 20% of features, making 'lite' products unsellable.
